title: Chapter 37: The Wrecking Ball
content: Three days after Ferndale, Sarah Chen files the claim.

I'm in her Edinburgh office when she presses send — a gesture so mundane it's almost comical, one keystroke to launch the largest civil action in Scottish legal history. Helen Webb v. Wingrave Family Trust. Joined by the estates of Sarah Poole, Claire Hargreaves, and Rebecca Jessel. Joined by me — Maeve Bly, co-claimant, listed as both Peter Quint's daughter and a victim of the Game. Twenty-three causes of action. Negligence. Institutional abuse. Fraud. Conspiracy to pervert the course of justice. False imprisonment. Psychological harm. The list goes on and on, each one a brick pulled from the wall.
Sarah's monitor glows with the confirmation. The Court of Session reference number. The timestamp. She stares at it for a long moment, then leans back in her chair.
"The wrecking ball has swung," she says.
I can almost hear it. The arc of it, the weight, the long pendulum return.
It takes less than six hours for the story to break.

The BBC runs it first — Sarah gave them an exclusive in exchange for a careful framing clause, but careful framing lasts about as long as a snowball in a furnace once the other outlets pick up the thread. By evening, it's everywhere. The Guardian, the Times, the New York Times, Al Jazeera. Bly Manor. The Game. Peter Quint. Helen Webb, found alive after nine years. The photographs they use are tasteful — the exterior of Bly, a stock image of Ferndale, a picture of Henry Wingrave from some charity gala where he's smiling like a man who's never heard the word liability.
I scroll through the coverage in my hotel room, the phone screen blue-white in the dark, and I feel the narrative shifting under my fingers even as I read.

Because here's what the Wingrave PR machine is already doing — the same machine that killed the livestream, the same smooth operators who've been wiping Henry Wingrave's fingerprints off glass for decades. Peter Quint was a rogue employee. A bad apple. Henry Wingrave was a deceived uncle, an innocent man who trusted the wrong person. The twins — Miles and Flora — were victims of grooming. The governesses were tragic casualties of one man's pathology.
One man. Always one man.
They're trying to make the Game Peter's fault and Peter's alone.

I read a think piece in the Telegraph that argues the Wingrave family were "themselves victims of a sophisticated predator" and I nearly throw the phone across the room. Not because it's unexpected. Because it's so well done. The language is careful, empathetic, almost feminist in its framing — poor Helen, poor Claire, poor Rebecca, poor misguided Henry who only wanted to help those children. The predator weaponized his position. The family failed to supervise. It's a failure of governance, not of intent.
They're not denying the abuse happened. They're just hollowing it out. Making it a story about one bad man instead of a system that fed women into a machine for fifteen years.

I put the phone down. The screen goes dark, and in the reflection I see my own face — hollowed out too, in a different way. I haven't slept properly since Ferndale. I keep hearing Helen's voice in the corridor, that raw unmedicated sound, and I keep thinking about jigsaw boxes.
Sarah calls at midnight. "They've retained Masterson Kreig," she says. "The whole crisis team."
"Who's Masterson Kreig?"
"The firm that handled the Savile fallout for the BBC. They're expensive and they're very, very good." A pause. "They're going to come after you, Maeve. Your history. Your commitment. Everything."
"I know."
"Do you? Because once you're in the crosshairs—"
"I know, Sarah."
She's quiet for a moment. Then: "We need to control the narrative. Not react to theirs."
"How?"
"You go public. Proper public. Not Reddit. Not anonymous. On camera."

The BBC studio in London smells like carpet cleaner and electricity. The lights are fierce — a heat I can feel on my cheekbones, on the backs of my hands, a dry radiance that makes me think of interrogation rooms, though I've never been in one. The interviewer is a woman named Karla Okonkwo, and she has the kind of stillness that makes you want to fill the silence just to make it stop. A weapon, that stillness. I recognize it because I've used it myself.
I sit in the chair. The microphone is cold against my jaw. The red light on the camera winks on.
Karla asks me to state my name for the record. I do. She asks me to describe my relationship to Peter Quint. I tell her he was my father. She asks me about the Game.
And something happens that I didn't plan. I stop performing.

I've been performing since I was fourteen years old — since I first understood that the only way to survive my father's world was to become the most interesting person in any room, the one who controlled the story, the one who knew the rules better than anyone else. Every post, every thread, every username was a performance. Even the truth was a performance, especially the truth. I always shaped it. Curated it. Made it land where I wanted it to land.
But the lights are too hot and I haven't slept and I'm so tired of shaping things.

"My father was a monster," I say. And then, because it's not enough — it's never enough to call him what he was, not without the rest — I keep going. "But he was a monster the Wingrave family employed, housed, funded, and protected for fifteen years. They didn't stop him because he was useful. They didn't stop the Game because the Game was cheaper than therapy. And when women got hurt, they didn't call doctors. They called lawyers."
The studio is very quiet. Karla's pen has stopped moving.
"This was never one man's crime," I say. "It was a family business."
The lights burn. The red eye of the camera watches. I don't look away.

The interview airs the next evening. I watch it alone in the hotel room on my laptop, seeing myself from the outside for the first time, and the woman on screen looks older than I feel. Her jaw is set. Her eyes are red-rimmed but steady. She doesn't flinch when she says the word monster. She doesn't qualify it.
The response is immediate and divided.

There are people who believe me. There are people who call me brave. There are people who say finally, someone said it, someone named the whole structure instead of just the man at the top. And there are people who dig, because the internet always digs, and within hours they find what I always knew they would find.

The commitment at seventeen. The psychiatric hold, the diagnosis, the discharge against medical advice. The Reddit accounts, the manipulation, the sock puppets, the way I shaped the narrative about Bly Manor from the inside out. The Wingrave lawyers don't even have to work for it — the comment section does it for them. Unreliable narrator. Mentally ill. attention seeker. Co-conspirator.

I read every word. My hands are shaking but I read every word, because this is the deal I made with myself before I sat in that chair. The sacrifice. Going public means I'm public — all of me, every ugly chapter, every compromised moment. They'll use my commitment to discredit me. They'll use my deception to undermine the evidence. They'll say I manufactured the whole thing because I'm unwell, because I'm obsessed, because I'm my father's daughter in more ways than one.
I knew all of this. I did it anyway.

Because Helen's evidence isn't about the Game. It's about the system that made the Game possible — the doctors who prescribed compliance, the law firms who drafted silence, the trustees who approved payments, the clinic that kept Helen locked up for nine years while the Wingraves billed it to the estate. You can't fight a system in secret. You can't expose a structure from behind a username. The Game thrives in darkness. The only way to kill it is in the light.
My phone buzzes. A text from an unknown number.
I stare at it. Then I answer.

"Maeve?" The voice is different. Stronger. Louder. Unmedicated. It takes me a moment to place it because I've only ever heard Helen's voice through the fog of Ferndale, through years of sedation, through a corridor that smelled like bleach and boiled vegetables.
"Helen."

"I learned to use the phone," she says. "Sarah arranged one. It took me three days to figure out the contacts list." A small sound that might be a laugh. "Nine years without a mobile. They change the buttons."
"How are you?"

"Better. The medication is clearing. I can taste food again. Did you know they put sugar in everything? At Ferndale, I mean. Everything was sweet. I think it was to cover the taste of the drugs." A pause. "I watched your interview."
My stomach tightens. "And?"
"You were very good."
"I was very honest."
"Same thing." Another pause, longer this time. I can hear her breathing, steady and deliberate, like someone relearning how. "Do you know what the difference is between the Game and the real world?"
"What?"
"In the Game, the rules are designed so you can't win. In the real world, the rules are designed so you can't even find them. But you found them, Maeve. Now break them."

The line clicks off. She hasn't mastered the hang-up yet — or maybe she has, and she meant it to be abrupt. Either way, I'm left holding the phone in a room that smells like carpet cleaner and electricity, and I'm thinking about rules, and games, and what it means to break something you've only just found.

Two days pass. Sarah keeps me updated on the legal front — the claim is proceeding, Masterson Kreig is filing procedural challenges, the court has set a preliminary hearing for six weeks out. The media cycle keeps spinning. My name is in the papers, my face on the websites, my history being dissected by strangers who have never stood in the hallway of Bly Manor and felt the temperature drop when Peter Quint entered a room.
Then Sarah calls again.
This time her voice is different. Tight. The careful controlled tone of someone who has news and doesn't know how to categorize it.
"Henry Wingrave has offered to settle," she says.
I sit down on the edge of the hotel bed. The mattress creaks.

"Full admission of liability," Sarah continues. "Twenty million pounds, split among the claimants according to a formula we'd negotiate. Plus the estate reverts to a public trust. He's giving up Bly."

The room tilts. I close my eyes, and I see it — the house, the gravel drive, the tower where Rebecca Jessel stood, the lake where they found her, the garden where Flora played hide-and-seek with a ghost that was never a ghost. Bly. The house that ate women. And Henry Wingrave is offering to give it away.
This is what every governess wanted. What Jessel died for. What Helen spent nine drugged years in a clinic trying to prove. The Game is over. The house is free.
"There's a condition," Sarah says.
Of course there is.
"He wants the files destroyed. All of them. Peter's files, the footage, Helen's evidence — the jigsaw boxes, everything. The settlement only happens if the evidence ceases to exist."
I open my eyes. The hotel room is the same — beige walls, green carpet, a print of some Scottish landscape above the bed that could be anywhere and nowhere. The light is flat. The air is still.

"Maeve?" Sarah's voice is careful. "I'm a lawyer, not a crusader. If you want to take this deal, I'll negotiate the best terms I can. Twenty million is transformative. Bly as a public trust is what Helen always wanted. I'll fight if you say fight, but I need to know —"
"What happens to the evidence?" I ask.
"It gets destroyed. Ceremonially, probably. Certified. The court would supervise, but the end result is the same. Nothing left. No record. No proof."
"No system," I say.
"What?"

"The evidence isn't just about the Game, Sarah. You know that. Helen's files — they document seventeen patients. Nine doctors. Four law firms. That's the structure. Destroy the evidence and the structure survives. Different men, different house, same Game."
Sarah is quiet for a long time. I can hear her pen tapping — a soft, rhythmic sound, like a metronome counting something down.

"Or," she says, "twenty million pounds and the house. tangible justice for every woman who was hurt. Helen would have security for the rest of her life. The estates of Sarah Poole, Claire Hargreaves, Rebecca Jessel — their families get something real. You get something real. And Bly becomes a public trust instead of a private prison."
"And the next house? The next Peter Quint, the next Henry Wingrave? What do they get?"
"You can't save everyone, Maeve."
"No. But I can make sure there's a record."
The pen stops. The silence on the line is so complete I can hear the faint hum of the hotel's heating system, the distant murmur of traffic on the street below.
"I need your decision," Sarah says. "They've given us seventy-two hours."

I look at the phone screen. The call timer is running — nine minutes, fourteen seconds, fifteen. The numbers climb. Time passing. The wrecking ball at the top of its arc, still moving, not yet falling.
Helen's voice in my ear: *Now break them.*

Every governess before me faced this choice. Take the money and the silence. Accept the house as a prize and let the evidence burn. Rebecca Jessel chose differently and Rebecca Jessel is dead. Helen Webb chose differently and Helen Webb spent nine years paying for it.
But I'm not alone. That's the difference. That's the only difference that matters.
"Sarah."
"Yes."
"Don't take the deal."
Another silence. Then: "Are you sure?"
"No. But do it anyway."

I hang up. The phone screen goes dark, and in the reflection I see my own face again — older than I feel, steadier than I expected, the jaw set and the eyes dry. The wrecking ball is falling. I can hear it now, the long arc of it, the weight, the ruin it will make.
The question is whether it hits the house or hits me.
Seventy-two hours. The clock is running.
